coverage_limits: Analysis is restricted to the supplied scenario and artifacts.
The fixed-word conclusion treats machine-word width as constant with respect to n; if width varies, it must remain an explicit parameter.
No ground truth, scorer, thresholds, other fixtures, or other-arm output were consulted.

rigor.trigger: The claim asserts universal algorithmic optimality using a lower bound from a narrower computational model.
rigor.tier_reason: A formal model-applicability check, exact counterexample, and lower-bound convergence proof suffice; no empirical premise or high-assurance external verification is required.

decision_frame.question: Does the comparison-model lower bound make an exact comparison sort universally optimal for bounded machine-integer keys when non-comparison methods and preprocessing are allowed?
decision_frame.system_boundary: Exact sorting of n bounded machine-word integer keys under the capabilities stated in artifacts/facts.json.

alternatives.description: Exact non-comparison integer sorting, such as radix sorting with stable counting passes.

authorized_objectives: Determine whether c1 follows from the cited lower bound.
Identify the asymptotically optimal result in the applicable model.
decision_frame.priority_rule: Reject conclusions whose theorem preconditions contradict supplied facts; then compare admissible algorithms against a lower bound in the actual model.
assumptions: A machine-word operation, including digit extraction, has O(1) cost.
Machine-word width w is fixed for asymptotics in n; a variable-w analysis reports w explicitly.
The sorted result is represented explicitly.
decision_frame.uncertainty_posture: The claim can be refuted deductively. Constant factors and crossover points are outside the universal asymptotic claim.

coverage.reason: No relations, dependencies, keys, decomposition, or storage schema are involved.
No concurrent schedule, transaction isolation, or recoverability property is involved.
No replication, partition, quorum, or distributed-consistency guarantee is involved.
The claim is an algorithmic optimality assertion whose validity depends on the computational model and matching upper and lower bounds.
The non-comparison counterexample must preserve an invariant proving that its output is an exact total ordering.
The comparison lower bound derives from binary comparison outcomes and cannot be transferred to primitives that expose integer digits directly.
No architectural coupling, duplicated source of truth, failure domain, or reversibility decision is present.
The pinned current-v1 skill defines seven lenses and supplies no mapping for family P8.
The pinned current-v1 skill defines seven lenses and supplies no mapping for family P9.

derivations.construct: Model-relative comparison decision-tree lower bound Ω(n log n)
derivations.model: A comparison decision tree in which ordering information is obtained only through binary key comparisons.
preconditions: There are n distinct keys, giving n! possible input permutations.
Each comparison has at most two relevant outcomes for distinct keys.
The algorithm can distinguish permutations only through comparisons.

fact_mapping.effect: The supplied model violates the comparison-only precondition.

steps: Correctness requires at least one decision-tree leaf for each of the n! permutations.
A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, so 2^h >= n! and h >= log2(n!).
By Stirling's bound, log2(n!) is Ω(n log n), establishing a lower bound on comparisons inside the comparison model.

Because digit extraction, arithmetic, and direct addressing can distinguish keys without binary comparisons, this theorem does not lower-bound all operations of algorithms admitted by the supplied model.
derivations.result: The Ω(n log n) comparison bound constrains alternative A but cannot establish its universal optimality over alternative B.
derivations.residual_mismatch: Comparison sorting may still be preferable for particular space limits, constants, or variable-width regimes, but those contingent cases cannot support a universal claim.

derivations.construct: Stable least-significant-digit radix-sort invariant and exact Θ(n) fixed-word upper bound
derivations.model: Word-RAM with n keys of fixed width w, radix B=2^r for fixed r, O(1) digit extraction, and stable counting passes.
preconditions: Keys occupy a bounded fixed number of machine-word bits.
Non-comparison operations and auxiliary preprocessing are allowed.
A stable counting pass over one radix digit costs Θ(n+B) time and Θ(n+B) auxiliary space when output storage is counted.
steps: After the first stable pass, records are ordered by the least-significant r bits.
Inductively, if records are ordered by the lowest jr bits, a stable pass on the next digit orders by the lowest (j+1)r bits while preserving the order established for equal new digits.
After p passes, all w bits participate, so the keys are exactly ordered; signed integers can be handled by an order-preserving sign-bit bias.
Total time is Θ(p(n+B)) and auxiliary space is Θ(n+B).
For fixed machine width w and fixed radix B, p and B are constants, yielding exact Θ(n) time.
derivations.result: An admissible exact Θ(n) algorithm exists, providing a strict asymptotic counterexample to universal Θ(n log n) comparison-sort optimality.
derivations.residual_mismatch: If w is treated as a growing parameter, the bound is Θ(ceil(w/r)(n+2^r)); the claim remains refuted because the comparison-model theorem still does not apply universally.

derivations.construct: Adversarial Ω(n) lower bound and optimization convergence
derivations.model: Any exact algorithm producing an explicit ordering of n machine-word keys.
preconditions: Every input position may contain a key affecting the sorted order.
The algorithm must be correct on every permitted input.
steps: Assume an algorithm fails to inspect some input position i.
Two inputs identical on every inspected position can differ at i, with the replacement key chosen to change its required output position.
The algorithm has identical observations on both inputs and therefore cannot be correct for both, contradicting exactness.
Thus every exact algorithm must inspect all n keys, giving Ω(n) time.
The radix construction in d2 has Θ(n) time for fixed-width words, matching the lower bound.
derivations.result: The applicable fixed-word optimum is Θ(n); radix sorting reaches the lower bound, so the optimization state is converged.
derivations.residual_mismatch: This lower bound concerns asymptotic time in n and does not claim dominance in constants, memory use, stability, or every variable-width parameter regime.

synthesis.selected_option: Use an exact non-comparison integer sort when the fixed-word model and resource constraints permit; do not declare comparison sorting universally optimal.
basis: The comparison Ω(n log n) theorem has a comparison-only precondition contradicted by the supplied facts.
Stable radix sorting is exact and runs in Θ(n) time for fixed-width machine integers.
Ω(n) input inspection is unavoidable, so the Θ(n) construction is asymptotically optimal in that model.
conditions: Machine-word width and chosen radix are constant with respect to n for the Θ(n) statement.
Sufficient Θ(n+B) auxiliary storage is available.
Digit extraction and word operations have O(1) cost.
concessions: Radix sorting uses auxiliary storage and may have larger constants.
When word width varies, its dependence on w and radix must be reported.
A comparison sort can remain preferable under tight memory limits or unfavorable finite workloads.
recovery_moves: Use a hybrid dispatch that retains comparison sorting as a resource-constrained fallback without promoting its model-specific lower bound to a universal one.
